sections.title: Global Equities
sections.content: Global equities fell as a renewed sell-off in mega-cap technology and AI-related shares dragged indices lower, triggered by a cautious Broadcom AI-chip outlook, Apple and Microsoft hardware price increases tied to rising memory costs and a report that OpenAI may delay its IPO to 2027. MSCI World fell roughly **2.6%**, MSCI Developed Markets around **2.5%** (approx., verify before merging) and MSCI Emerging Markets about **3%** (approx., verify before merging) as Korean chipmakers slid. The S&P 500 lost about **2.0%** to roughly **7,354** and the Nasdaq dropped more than **5%**, the MAG7 cohort leading declines, while the Dow finished broadly flat. Europe's Euro Stoxx 50 fell about **1.2%** to near **6,212**. In Asia the Nikkei 225 ended roughly flat (about **+0.4%**) after a steep Friday plunge, China's CSI 300 declined around **2%** (approx., verify before merging) and Hong Kong's Hang Seng eased about **1.5%** (approx., verify before merging).

sections.title: US Equities
sections.content:
US indices finished mixed-to-lower as renewed weakness in large-cap technology weighed on the broad market. The Nasdaq Composite posted a fifth straight losing session and fell more than **5%** on the week, while the S&P 500 slid about **2.0%** to around **7,354**. A rotation into defensives left the Dow broadly flat to higher, supported by lower oil prices and easing bond yields.

The sell-off was driven by deteriorating AI sentiment: a cautious Broadcom outlook on AI-chip demand, a report that OpenAI may delay its IPO, and Apple and Microsoft price increases on select hardware amid rising memory costs combined to trigger a chip-stock rout. Strong results from Micron could not stem the broader de-rating of mega-cap tech.

Macro data were firmer. The flash composite PMI rose to a five-month high of **52.2**, manufacturing reached **55.7**, its strongest since 2022, and services edged up to **51.3**. Employment, however, softened for a second straight month as firms focused on cost control amid still-elevated input-price inflation.

sections.title: European Equities
sections.content:
European equities were broadly flat as an early-week advance gave way to a Friday technology sell-off driven by concerns over stretched AI-related valuations. The pan-European STOXX Europe 600 ended essentially unchanged, up **0.04%** in local-currency terms, while the Euro Stoxx 50 fell about **1.2%** to near **6,212**, masking wide regional dispersion.

The UK's FTSE 100 outperformed on its value and energy tilt, while Germany's DAX, France's CAC 40 and Italy's market lagged. Stock-specific moves were sharp: Volkswagen slid **3.9%** on reports of potential job cuts and plant closures, Siemens Energy dropped **5.6%** and Infineon fell **4.3%**, while banks including Deutsche Bank, down **2.7%**, and BNP Paribas came under pressure.

Lower oil prices offered some relief to the region's net importers and eased the inflation impulse, but the global repricing of technology and AI exposure dominated sentiment into the close, leaving energy and defensive-tilted markets relatively better placed.

sections.title: APAC Equities
sections.content:
APAC markets were weaker as the global technology and AI sell-off swept the region. Japan's Nikkei 225 ended roughly flat on the week, up about **0.4%**, having rallied early before a Friday plunge of more than **4%** as chip-related names and SoftBank tumbled. South Korea's Kospi was the epicentre, falling close to **10%** on the week with circuit breakers triggered as Samsung Electronics and SK Hynix each shed more than **10%** at one point.

China's CSI 300 slid around **2.4%** on Friday and Hong Kong's Hang Seng fell about **1.6%**, with investors still awaiting fresh domestic stimulus, while the MSCI Asia ex-Japan Index dropped nearly **2.9%** on the day.

Falling oil offered a partial offset for the region's energy importers. With the AI-driven tech cycle the dominant swing factor, regional risk appetite tracked the deteriorating mood in global mega-cap technology rather than domestic fundamentals.

sections.title: Foreign Exchange
sections.content:
The US dollar extended its rally, with the DXY clearing the **100.50** ceiling to a one-year high as widening rate differentials reasserted themselves as the dominant FX driver. Persistent US inflation and resilient data reinforced a hawkish Fed, while softer eurozone growth and cooling prices reduced ECB tightening pressure.

EUR/USD broke below **1.14**, finding buyers near **1.1350** and leaving the euro down more than **2.5%** month-to-date, its largest monthly fall in almost a year; Convera flagged a firmer but choppy dollar into early Q3 absent a fresh catalyst.

Sterling and the yen also ceded ground to broad dollar strength, with USD/JPY pushing beyond **161**. The greenback's momentum overrode the risk-off equity flows that might otherwise have lent the traditional haven currencies support.

sections.title: Commodities
sections.content:
Energy led commodities lower, the dominant theme as improving US-Iran relations brightened the supply outlook, increased tanker traffic through the Strait of Hormuz and deflated the geopolitical risk premium. WTI fell more than **4%** towards **USD 70**, its lowest since early March, with Brent easing in sympathy.

In metals, gold held firm near **USD 4,200**, supported by lower oil prices and a softer dollar earlier in the week after the prior week's hawkish FOMC meeting. Copper broke key support at **USD 6.15** to a seven-week low, triggering technical selling and hedge-fund long liquidation on a stronger dollar and the Fed's hawkish stance.

The cross-asset read-through was disinflationary: cheaper energy eased near-term inflation concerns and lent support to bonds, even as the broad commodity complex traded softer on the week.

sections.title: Fixed Income
sections.content:
Government bonds firmed as moderating inflation expectations relieved pressure on rates, a shift from the prior week's hawkish-Fed sell-off. The 10-year UST yield hovered around **4.4%**, easing to roughly **4.41%** by Friday's close, with lower oil reinforcing the move.

Edward Jones noted that cheaper energy, with WTI near **USD 70**, should moderate headline inflation in the coming months, tempering expectations for further Fed tightening even as policy stays restrictive.

Credit tone was mixed. Investment-grade corporates gained, with new issues generally oversubscribed, while high yield came under more pressure amid concerns over monetary policy, AI-driven equity valuations, heavy new supply, slowing fund inflows and a broader risk-off mood that mirrored the week's renewed equity-market caution.
